questions.question: What is a Python module? Explain its significance in developing IoT applications.
questions.hint: Focus on reusability and organization benefits for IoT projects
keyPoints: A module is a file containing Python definitions and statements
It has .py extension and can be imported into other programs
In IoT: separates sensor drivers, data processing, communication logic
Prevents monolithic code in large IoT projects

questions.question: Explain the different ways to import modules in Python with syntax and examples.
questions.hint: Give practical examples for each import style
keyPoints: from module_name import specific_item - imports specific items
from module_name import * - imports all items (not recommended)
import module_name as alias - imports with alias name
from module_name import item as alias - imports item with alias

questions.question: What is the module search path in Python? How does sys.path influence module importing?
questions.hint: Mention sys.path list and its components
keyPoints: sys.path is a list of directories searched for modules
Can be modified at runtime to add custom paths
Python searches in order: current folder → PYTHONPATH → standard library

questions.question: Explain the __name__ == '__main__' pattern. Why is it important for module design?
questions.hint: Give example showing different behavior when imported vs run directly
keyPoints: __name__ is a special variable set by Python interpreter
Allows code to run only when file is executed, not when imported

questions.question: List and explain any five built-in Python modules useful for IoT development.
questions.hint: Focus on relevance of each module to IoT applications
keyPoints: json: JSON data handling for IoT communication
socket: network programming for IoT connectivity

questions.question: How do you create and use a custom module in Python? Explain with a practical example.
keyPoints: Save in Python path or current directory

questions.question: Design a modular IoT application structure for a Weather Monitoring System. Show how you would organize code into separate modules with specific responsibilities.
questions.hint: Show actual module names and their functions
keyPoints: Module 1: sensor_module - reads temperature, humidity, pressure
Module 2: data_processing - converts, validates, aggregates data

questions.question: Explain the differences between import, from...import, and import...as statements. When should each be used in IoT applications?
questions.hint: Provide code examples for each case
keyPoints: import module: imports everything, use module.function()
from module import item: imports specific items directly
import module as alias: creates alias for module name
from module import item as alias: creates alias for item
Use import when many items needed, avoid namespace pollution
Use from...import when only few items needed
Use aliases for long module names or name conflicts

questions.question: Write a Python program to create a module named 'sensor_utils' with functions for (i) reading temperature in Celsius and converting to Fahrenheit, (ii) reading humidity and validating range. Demonstrate importing and using this module.
keyPoints: Show calling functions with sample values

questions.question: Explain the __all__ attribute in Python modules. How does it control what gets imported with 'from module import *'?
questions.hint: Give example showing difference with and without __all__
keyPoints: __all__ is a list defining public API of module
When defined, only items in __all__ are imported with *

questions.question: Consider a Python program with the following module structure: main.py imports sensor.py which imports calibration.py. If calibration.py has a function 'adjust_reading(value, offset)' and sensor.py imports it as 'adj', show how to call this function from main.py using all three import methods.
keyPoints: Method 2: from sensor import adj → adj.adjust_reading(x, y)
Method 3: from calibration import adjust_reading (in sensor) → sensor uses it
Show actual import statements in each file
Show function call syntax from main

questions.question: Given the following module 'iot_logger.py': (i) Write code to create a Logger class with log_message() method, (ii) Add __all__ to export only Logger class, (iii) Show how to import this module to use the Logger in another file.
keyPoints: class Logger with __init__ and log_message methods
import iot_logger or from iot_logger import Logger

questions.question: What is the difference between a Python module and a Python package? How does __init__.py make a directory a package?
questions.hint: Relate to hierarchical IoT project structure
keyPoints: Package is a directory containing multiple modules
Can contain __init__.py which runs when package imported

questions.question: Write Python code to demonstrate how to use the time and datetime modules for timestamp generation in an IoT data logging application. Show creating timestamp and formatted date string.
keyPoints: import time - time.time() for Unix timestamp
import datetime - datetime.now() for current datetime
Show output for IoT sensor reading with timestamp

questions.question: Design and write Python code for an IoT data collection module that (i) defines functions to read temperature and humidity from sensors, (ii) uses json module to format data, (iii) uses os module to create log files. Show complete module implementation.
questions.hint: Integrate multiple modules (json, os, time) in one module
keyPoints: format_data() uses json.dumps() for JSON formatting
log_data() uses os module for file operations
